items.correct: A cadence ending on chord V, creating a sense of incompleteness.
items.misconception: A cadence that sounds wrong or is performed badly.
items.random_wrong: A cadence using only major chords.
items.why: Students think 'imperfect' means flawed. It's a technical term for an unfinished-sounding cadence - any chord moving to V leaves the music suspended, awaiting resolution.

items.q: What does 'piano' (p) mean as a dynamic marking?
items.why: Students see 'piano' and think of the instrument. As dynamic, p = soft (from Italian). The instrument is actually a shortened 'pianoforte' (soft-loud) because it could play at varying dynamics, unlike the harpsichord.

items.q: How does D Dorian differ from D minor?
items.correct: Dorian has a raised 6th (B natural) compared to natural minor.
items.random_wrong: Dorian has no sharps or flats at all.
items.why: Students treat modes as variants of major/minor. Dorian on D uses only white keys (like C major, but starting on D), giving a raised 6th compared to D natural minor - producing the modal flavour.
Correct answer: Schoenberg. “Stravinsky” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which composer is most associated with minimalist works such as 'Music for 18 Musicians'?
items.why: Correct answer: Reich. “Glass” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year did John Cage compose his silent work '4'33"'?
items.why: Exact answer: 1952. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
